raw_case:
Significance of ultrasonography in diagnosing transverse testicular ectopia: A case report
An uncommon congenital abnormality known as crossed testicular ectopia (CTE) or transverse testicular ectopia (TTE) occurs when both testes migrate into the same hemiscrotum. Inguinal hernia and persistent Müllerian duct syndrome are frequently associated with it. We present a case of “TTE” in a 1-year-old boy with an empty right hemiscrotum and left inguinal hernia. TTE, first described by Von Lenhossek, can be diagnosed via ultrasound, with over 100 cases reported. Theories of its origin include developmental anomalies of the genital ridge and Wolffian duct fusion. Accurate diagnosis is crucial due to increased malignancy risks and complications in management.

Introduction

A rare congenital abnormality known as CTE or TTE occurs when both testes migrate into the same hemiscrotum. Other abnormalities include scrotal irregularities, hypospadias, true hermaphroditism, pseudo hermaphroditism, inguinal hernia, and persistent Müllerian duct syndrome [1,2]. Research has recorded around a hundred cases of transverse testicular ectopia [3]. We have reported a case of TTE in a 1-year-old boy who had an empty right hemiscrotum and only a left inguinal hernia. TTE is a rare condition where both testes pass through or descend a single inguinal canal or hemiscrotum. It is also known as testicular pseudoduplication, transverse aberrant testicular mal-descent, and unilateral double testis [4]. Ultrasound examinations often make the diagnosis, and medical literature has documented over 100 instances of TTE [5]. The current study details an unexpected discovery of a TTE case during an ultrasound examination for a left inguinal hernia.

Case presentation

Since birth, the 1-year-old boy had a nontender, mobile, firm, and persistent swelling in the left inguinal region. During the external genitalia assessment, the left testis was palpable in the left hemiscrotum with swelling in the left inguinal area, while the right hemiscrotum was empty. He was a term baby born via normal vaginal delivery with a satisfactory Apgar score. There were no prior illnesses, feeding issues, or signs of failure to thrive (FTT). The general physical examination did not reveal any noteworthy findings. Haematological and biochemical analyses showed normal results. The patient underwent an ultrasound examination due to a nonpalpable right testis and a left inguinal hernia.Ultrasonography (USG) revealed the following findings: Fig. 1 depicts the transverse section of the left hemiscrotum, where the left testis displays normal size and echotexture. Transverse and sagittal sections of a USG scan of the left inguinal area show that there is swelling and what looks like an ectopic (right) testis in the left inguinal area, with only a small amount of fluid surrounding it (Fig. 2). Fig. 3 shows an oblique view of USG in the left deep inguinal ring with minimal free fluid.Fig. 4 displays common cord structures with 2 testes, but it is not possible to distinguish between the right and left testes. Fig. 5, Fig. 6 demonstrate a power Doppler study that shows normal vascularity of the left and ectopic testis, respectively. The child underwent simultaneous bilateral orchiopexy and herniotomy on the left side. During surgery, the scrotal sac on the right side was empty, the testis on the left side was in the left hemi-scrotal sac, and there was a moving mass (probably an ectopic testis) in the left inguinal area Fig. 7A.During the laparoscopic procedure, we observed the right testis in the left inguinal canal. Fig. 7B shows the location of the left testis and gubernaculum in the left hemiscrotum sac, while Fig. 7(C) shows the opening of the common cord structure. We entered and visualised the peritoneal cavity through the laparoscopic approach. But no testicular tissue or spermatic cord was found. The right testis and its covering tunica vaginalis were subsequently discovered at the deep inguinal ring. Upon opening the tunica, fluid drained out, revealing the testis. We located the left testis at the other end of the cord. This confirmed the transverse testicular ectopia on the left side. We then adjusted the treatment plan to include cord release and bilateral orchiopexy. The 2 cords shared a common origin for a length of 3–4 cm. We released the right cord at its bifurcation site, completing the dissection distally. Finally, we seamlessly transferred the right testis and its cord to the right hemiscrotum extraperitoneally. We secured both testes in the sub-dartos pouch (Fig. 8).

Discussion

Transverse testicular ectopia (TTE) represents a rare variation of testicular ectopia, with its initial documentation dating back to Von Lenhossek in 1886 [6]. Over 100 documented cases of TTE have been recorded in medical literature [7]. Various theories have been proposed to elucidate the origins of TTE, such as Berg's suggestion of both testes developing from a shared genital ridge [8], Kimura's observation regarding the crossing over of 1 testis due to the origin of both vasa differentia [9], and Gupta and Das's hypothesis involving early adherence and fusion of Wolffian ducts leading to the sequential descent of the testes [10].In men, the testicles are the gonads that make sperm and androgen. They are part of the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis and play a dual role in hormone production. Normally, the testicles are smooth, soft, oval organs located in the scrotum and separated by the scrotal septum, measuring 3-5 cm in length and 2-3 cm in width. The spermatic cord and the scrotal ligament (embryo, logically the gubernaculum) suspend the testes. The tunica vaginalis, a double-layered structure, covers the testes except at the posterior and superior borders, where the epididymis and spermatic cord attach. The tunica albuginea is a thick, fibrous sheath located beneath the tunica vaginalis [11].Testicular development starts with the formation of the genital ridge from the intermediate mesoderm. The genital ridge is made up of coelomic epithelial cells that grow on the ventromedial surface of the mesonephros. The mesonephros has the Wolffian duct, which is part of the male reproductive tract and connects to the epididymis, seminal vesicles, and vas deferens. If there are no male sex determination genes, the Müllerian duct runs parallel to the uterus and leads to the uterus, fallopian tubes, and vagina, which are all reproductive organs for women [12].Primordial germ cells (PGCs), originating near the yolk sac, colonize genital ridges by the sixth week of gestation. These cells specialize as gametogenesis-competent cells, responding to cues from mesonephric tissue. In male embryos (XY), the SRY gene on the Y chromosome starts the formation of the testis by making regulatory proteins. This causes Sertoli cells to differentiate. The SRY gene activates the SOX9 gene, crucial for Sertoli cell development and further testis formation.Testicular descent involves 2 phases: the transabdominal phase (eighth to 15th weeks) and the inguinoscrotal phase (from the 26th week). Initially, the gubernaculum enlarges and guides the testis towards the inguinal canal, influenced by nonandrogenic factors like Insl3. Androgens, the formation of the processes vaginalis, and intra-abdominal pressure facilitate the testis descent into the scrotum during the inguinoscrotal phase. The vaginal process usually obliterates later, preventing hernias. By the 33rd gestational week, most testes have descended into the scrotum, with only a small percentage descended by the 25th and 27th weeks.The gubernaculum test is essential for the descent of the testicles during fetal development. At first, the gubernaculum grows below the testis, holding it in place near the future inguinal area. Hormones like insulin-like factor 3 (Insl3) help this process along. During the transabdominal phase (8th to 15th weeks of gestation), the gubernaculum enlarges and shortens to guide the testis toward the internal inguinal ring. During the inguinoscrotal phase, which starts in the 26th week, testosterone helps the gubernaculum pull the testis through the inguinal canal and into the scrotum. This is made easier by pressure inside the abdomen and the formation of the processus vaginalis. Postdescent, the gubernaculum regresses, leaving remnants that secure the testis in the scrotum.The migration of the ectopic testis to a specific side typically corresponds with the presence of an inguinal hernia. Based on associated anomalies, we categorize TTE into 3 types: Type 1 primarily involves a hernia, Type 2 features persistent or rudimentary Müllerian duct structures, and Type 3 is associated with disorders beyond Müllerian remnants. Our case aligns with Type 1 TTE and underscores the rarity of TTE linked with fused vas deferens, which complicates orchiopexy procedures.The average age of presentation for TTE is around 4 years, with clinical manifestations commonly including an inguinal hernia on 1 side and contralateral or bilateral cryptorchidism. Accurate preoperative diagnosis of TTE often requires surgical exploration, highlighting the crucial role of tools such as ultrasound. Patients with TTE are at an elevated risk of gonadal malignancy, with reported outcomes including embryonal carcinoma, seminoma, yolk sac tumor, and teratoma. We advise surgical management via orchiopexy postdiagnosis, with laparoscopy proving beneficial in both diagnosis and treatment. We commonly employ surgical techniques like trans-septal or extra-peritoneal transposition orchiopexy, and recommend careful follow-up postoperatively [13,14]. We should carefully distinguish TTE from other conditions such as inguinal hernias or intersex variations, as prompt and accurate diagnosis is crucial for successful outcomes [15].

Conclusion

Patients with TTE are at an elevated risk of gonadal malignancy, with reported outcomes including embryonal carcinoma, seminoma, yolk sac tumor, and teratoma. We advise surgical management via orchiopexy postdiagnosis, with laparoscopy proving beneficial in both diagnosis and treatment.

Authors contributions

1st author – Dr. Govindarajan B. Rajendran, 2nd author and Corresponding author– Dr. Sharmeela Sukumar, 3rd author – Dr. Raam Ratish Gobinathan.

Declaration of generative AI and AI-assisted technologies in the writing process

We used OpenAI during the preparation of this work to improve language and readability. After using this tool/service, the author(s) reviewed and edited the content as needed and took full responsibility for the content of the publication.

Patient consent

The authors certify that they have obtained all appropriate patient consent.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** 1-year-old male child
- **Chief Complaint:** Persistent swelling in the left inguinal region and an empty right hemiscrotum since birth.
- **History of Present Illness:** Nontender, firm, mobile swelling in the left inguinal region; the left testis was palpable in the left hemiscrotum, while the right hemiscrotum was empty.
- **Past Medical History:** None
- **Personal History:** Born at term via vaginal delivery with a satisfactory Apgar score; no prior illnesses, feeding issues, or failure to thrive.
- **Family History:** None
- **Physical Examination:** Left testis palpable in the left hemiscrotum with a left inguinal swelling; empty right hemiscrotum; otherwise unremarkable examination.
- **Ancillary Tests:** 
  - Haematological and biochemical tests: Normal.
  - Ultrasonography: Normal left testis in the left hemiscrotum; swelling and an ectopic right testis with normal vascularity in the left inguinal area. 
  - Surgical exploration:
    - Empty right scrotal sac.
    - Right testis located in the left inguinal canal, confirming transverse testicular ectopia (TTE).
    - Common origin of the cords for 3–4 cm.
    - Laparoscopy: Clear visualization of the right testis and shared cord structures.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. The presence of swelling in the left inguinal region with an empty right hemiscrotum directed the initial differential diagnoses toward conditions causing cryptorchidism, such as:
   - **Inguinal Hernia with Cryptorchidism:** The coexistence of left inguinal swelling and nonpalpable right testis increased suspicion of this condition. However, surgical and ultrasound findings identified the ectopic right testis in the left inguinal canal.
   - **Transverse Testicular Ectopia (TTE):** Strongly suspected due to the findings of both testes being on the same side, confirmed by surgical exploration and ultrasound findings of a shared cord structure.
2. Exclusion of other possibilities:
   - Disorders of Sexual Development/Intersex Variants: No atypical genitalia, hormonal abnormalities, or Müllerian duct remnants were found during exploration, reducing the likelihood of intersex conditions or persistent Müllerian duct syndrome.
   - Tumor/Inguinal Mass: Unlikely as ultrasound and surgery showed a normal testicular echotexture and vascularity, without any evidence of malignancy or significant masses.
   - True Hermaphroditism: Excluded due to the lack of abnormal physical or imaging findings (e.g., Müllerian structures, ambiguous genitalia).
generate_case.final_diagnosis: The final diagnosis is **Type 1 Transverse Testicular Ectopia (TTE)** confirmed by clinical examination, ultrasonography showing two testes on the same side with a solitary inguinal hernia, and surgical exploration demonstrating a common spermatic cord and transverse position of the right testis into the left hemiscrotum.

results.thinking_process:
Okay, let's start by looking at the chief complaint. The baby has a persistent swelling in the left inguinal region and an empty right hemiscrotum since birth. The left testis is palpable in the scrotum, but the right side is empty. That makes me think about possible undescended testis on the right side. But wait, the swelling is on the left. So maybe the right testis didn't descend properly and is somewhere else.

The ultrasound showed the left testis is normal in position. But the right testis is ectopic in the left inguinal area. Wait, the right testis is on the left side? That's unusual. Typically, an undescended testis would be on the same side. If the right testis is in the left inguinal canal, this might be a case of transverse testicular ectopia (TTE). I remember TTE is when both testes descend through the same inguinal canal. So maybe both testes are on the left side here.

Surgical exploration confirmed the right testis in the left inguinal canal. Also, the cords have a common origin. Laparoscopy showed shared cord structures. TTE is often associated with other abnormalities like persistent Müllerian duct syndrome, but in this case, there's no mention of that. The family history is negative, so maybe it's an isolated TTE.

Other possibilities could be a retractile testis, but since it's been empty since birth and confirmed by imaging, that's less likely. Inguinal hernia might present with swelling, but the presence of the testis there points away from that. The key here is the ectopic testis crossing to the opposite side. The combination of an empty right scrotum, left inguinal swelling with both testes there, and surgical findings all point to TTE. The final diagnosis should be Transverse Testicular Ectopia.
results.content:
```
### Resoning:
<step 1> The patient presents with a left inguinal swelling and an empty right hemiscrotum since birth. The left testis is palpable in the scrotum, while the right hemiscrotum is empty, suggesting possible cryptorchidism on the right side. However, the presence of a left-sided swelling complicates the picture.

<step 2> Ultrasonography reveals an ectopic right testis with normal vascularity in the left inguinal area. This indicates that the right testis has migrated abnormally to the contralateral inguinal canal, ruling out simple unilateral cryptorchidism.

<step 3> Surgical exploration confirms the right testis is located in the left inguinal canal, with a common origin of the spermatic cords for 3–4 cm. This anatomical arrangement, along with laparoscopy findings of shared cord structures, aligns with transverse testicular ectopia (TTE), a rare condition where both testes descend through the same inguinal canal.

<step 4> The absence of other congenital anomalies (e.g., persistent Müllerian duct syndrome) and normal biochemical/haematological tests further narrows the diagnosis to isolated TTE. The combination of physical findings, imaging, and surgical observations definitively supports this conclusion.

### Answer:
Transverse Testicular Ectopia (TTE)

reasoning_eval.step: The patient presents with a left inguinal swelling and an empty right hemiscrotum since birth. The left testis is palpable in the scrotum, while the right hemiscrotum is empty, suggesting possible cryptorchidism on the right side. However, the presence of a left-sided swelling complicates the picture.

Ultrasonography reveals an ectopic right testis with normal vascularity in the left inguinal area. This indicates that the right testis has migrated abnormally to the contralateral inguinal canal, ruling out simple unilateral cryptorchidism.

Surgical exploration confirms the right testis is located in the left inguinal canal, with a common origin of the spermatic cords for 3–4 cm. This anatomical arrangement, along with laparoscopy findings of shared cord structures, aligns with transverse testicular ectopia (TTE), a rare condition where both testes descend through the same inguinal canal.

The absence of other congenital anomalies (e.g., persistent Müllerian duct syndrome) and normal biochemical/haematological tests further narrows the diagnosis to isolated TTE. The combination of physical findings, imaging, and surgical observations definitively supports this conclusion.

gt_reasoning_eval.step: <Step 1> Presence of swelling in the left inguinal region with an empty right hemiscrotum directed initial differential diagnoses towards conditions causing cryptorchidism, including Inguinal Hernia with Cryptorchidism and Transverse Testicular Ectopia (TTE).
<Step 2> Surgical and ultrasound findings identified the ectopic right testis in the left inguinal canal, excluding Inguinal Hernia with Cryptorchidism.
<Step 3> Findings of both testes on the same side strongly suspected Transverse Testicular Ectopia (TTE), confirmed by surgical exploration and ultrasound showing a shared cord structure.
<Step 4> Disorders of Sexual Development/Intersex Variants excluded due to absence of atypical genitalia, hormonal abnormalities, or Müllerian duct remnants.
<Step 5> Tumor/Inguinal Mass unlikely as ultrasound and surgery showed normal testicular echotexture and vascularity, without evidence of malignancy or significant masses.
<Step 6> True Hermaphroditism excluded due to lack of abnormal physical or imaging findings such as Müllerian structures or ambiguous genitalia.
<Step 7> Final diagnosis confirmed as Type 1 Transverse Testicular Ectopia (TTE) based on clinical examination, ultrasonography, and surgical exploration.
